title: Beeswax votive candles
subtitle: Plain poured votives with the natural honey scent of pure beeswax
excerpt: Small poured votive candles made from pure beeswax, relying on the natural honey fragrance of the wax rather than added essential oils. Beeswax burns longer and cleaner than paraffin or soy at the same weight. Pour time is around 20 minutes; the votives are ready to use in a few hours.
sourceNotes: Beeswax melt point: 62 to 65°C. Pour temperature for votives: 68 to 73°C to reduce pitting. Beeswax density and shrinkage: standard candle-making reference data from the British Beekeepers Association practical guides. Wick sizing for beeswax: beeswax requires a slightly larger wick than soy or paraffin for the same diameter due to higher viscosity.

recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. Beeswax sets quickly; prepare all moulds before starting.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Allow 6 hours before removing from moulds. Fully set votives can be stored for years.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Beeswax votives are effectively indefinite in storage. Beeswax does not go rancid. A white powdery bloom (bloom) may develop on the surface over time; this is harmless and disappears when the candle is lit or gently wiped with a soft cloth.

glossaryTerms.definition: For votive candles used in a votive holder, the first burn should last until the wax pool reaches the sides of the holder. This prevents tunnelling on subsequent burns.

A white powdery or chalky film that develops on the surface of beeswax candles over time, caused by the migration of certain wax components to the surface. It is harmless and does not affect burn quality.
The temperature at which a wax fully liquefies. Beeswax has a higher melt point (62 to 65°C) than most other candle waxes, which is why beeswax candles burn slowly and hold their shape in warm rooms.

content.text: Beeswax is the oldest candle material in continuous use. Its naturally high

(62 to 65°C) means beeswax candles burn slowly and steadily, with no dripping in a well-sized wick and a clean, bright flame. Pure beeswax has a gentle honey fragrance from the plant compounds in the wax; this formula relies on that natural scent rather than added oils. Beeswax votives are typically held in glass votive cups, which support the wax as it melts and contains the pool. They are quick to pour, quick to set, and keep effectively indefinitely. Each votive weighs about 25 g and gives around 4 hours of burn time in a standard votive holder.

items.prepNote: Yellow or white beeswax pellets; yellow has a stronger honey fragrance
Pre-tabbed wicks sized for a votive mould or small jar; ECO-4 or CD-10 for a 45 mm diameter

content.text: Set out the votive moulds or small heat-resistant cups. Secure one pre-tabbed wick at the centre of each mould. Warm the moulds slightly if they are cold glass; beeswax sets fast on contact with cold surfaces and can crack the sides of the mould or produce visible layering lines.

Weigh the beeswax pellets into the pouring pitcher. Melt over a water bath or very low direct heat, stirring occasionally. Do not leave melting wax unattended. Beeswax is more viscous than soy when molten and takes longer to melt fully; stir gently rather than rushing the heat. Heat to 70 to 75°C, checking with the probe thermometer. Beeswax poured at this temperature gives a smoother surface than wax poured at its minimum pour temperature.

Pour the molten beeswax into the prepared moulds in a slow, steady stream, filling to within a few millimetres of the top. Keep the wicks centred as you pour. Do not move the moulds while the wax is setting; beeswax sets faster than soy and any disturbance creates air pockets. As the wax cools, a shallow well forms around the wick. Top up with a small second pour of wax reheated to 70°C once the first pour has fully set (usually about 2 hours).
After 6 hours the votives will have contracted and released from the mould sides. Flex the mould gently to release, or tip onto a soft cloth. Trim the wick to 5 mm. A light white
may appear on the surface within a few weeks. This is the natural character of beeswax and does not affect burn quality.
Always burn votive candles in a heat-resistant votive holder that catches any wax pool. On the first burn, keep the candle lit until the

is complete and the pool has reached the edges of the holder. Beeswax requires a slightly larger wick than soy wax for the same diameter; if the flame is small and the wax pool does not reach the edges, try a larger wick size on the next batch.
Beeswax votives keep indefinitely in cool, dark storage. The wax does not degrade or go rancid. Surface bloom can be gently polished off with a soft cloth before use if desired.
